title: Spin Dynamics from Atomistic Quantum Simulations
authors: Enrico Drigo, Marquis M. McMillan, Benjamin Pingault, Yinan Dong, F. Joseph Heremans, David D. Awschalom, Giulia Galli
abstract: Optically active solid-state spin defects are promising candidates for quantum applications, however a unified theoretical framework to predict their spin dynamics at high temperatures is not yet available. Here, using Kubo linear--response theory, we derive expressions of spin-lattice and decoherence times \(T_1\) and \(T_2\) in terms of correlation functions of spin--lattice couplings. We then evaluate \(T_1\) and \(T_2\) from molecular dynamics and spin--lattice interaction time--series generated by state--of--the--art machine learning models trained on {\it ab--initio} data. Finally we measure \(T_1\) times for the NV center in diamond and compare experimental and theoretical results, showing excellent agreement.
summary: This paper presents a new method to simulate the spin dynamics of NV centers in diamond at high temperatures using machine learning-accelerated molecular dynamics. By applying Kubo linear-response theory, the authors accurately predict relaxation and decoherence times that match experimental results. This approach allows for the inclusion of complex anharmonic effects and large-scale lattice dynamics.
main_problem: The lack of a unified theoretical framework to predict spin dynamics, specifically spin-lattice relaxation (T1) and decoherence (T2), for solid-state spin defects at high temperatures.
main_result: The authors developed a framework using Kubo linear-response theory and machine learning-accelerated molecular dynamics that accurately predicts T1 and T2 times for NV centers in diamond, showing excellent agreement with experimental data.
method: The study combines Kubo linear-response theory with large-scale molecular dynamics simulations accelerated by MACE machine learning interatomic potentials and a neural network for computing ZFS tensors.
model_or_system: The physical system is the nitrogen-vacancy (NV) center in diamond. The model uses a spin Hamiltonian with zero-field splitting (ZFS) and accounts for spin-lattice interactions through fluctuations in the ZFS tensor.
key_observables: Spin-lattice relaxation time (T1), transverse decoherence time (T2), vibrational density of states (VDOS), and phonon dispersion.
important_parameters: Temperature range of 400 K to 1000 K; crossover from T5 to T2 scaling in T1 inverse at approximately 500 K; Debye temperature of diamond (~2000 K).
main_assumptions: The derivation assumes the high-temperature limit, linear order in external perturbation, and second order in the spin-lattice interaction term.
figures_summary: Figure 1 compares VDOS via ML-MD vs crystalline models; Figure 2 shows convergence analysis of T1 with respect to cell size; Figures 5 and 6 show analytical temperature scaling for spin-one and spin-two phonon processes.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the problem of high-temperature spin dynamics, derives a theoretical framework using Kubo LRT, describes the ML-accelerated MD and NN methods, presents validation against DFPT and experiments, and concludes with an analysis of phonon-driven scaling laws.
why_interesting: It provides a powerful bridge between atomistic simulations and open quantum system dynamics, specifically demonstrating how ML can enable the study of complex, anharmonic, many-body phonon-spin interactions that are otherwise computationally inaccessible.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: Figure 2. Convergence analysis of T1 as a function of the num- ber of atoms in the simulation cell and segment size used in the block analysis. All calculations are performed using 16 ns NVE molecular dynamics simulations with machine learned potentials at 500 K.

Figure 1. Left panel: comparison of the vibrational density of states (VDOS) of the NV center in diamond computed from molecular dynamics (MD) simulations with machine learned (ML) potentials in the dilute limit, and of crystalline dia- mond computed via density functional perturbation theory (DFPT) [38]. Right panel: comparison of the phonon dis- persion of the NV center in diamond computed from MD in the dilute limit and of crystalline diamond computed via DFPT [38]. The ML MD calculations are performed using a 2 ns NVE trajectory of one NV center in a diamond cell of 5832 atoms at 1000 K.

Figure 4. Spin-lattice relaxation, T1, (upper panel) and deco- herence time, T2, (lower panel) of the NV center in diamond as a function of temperature. T1 and T2 are evaluated using Eqs. (3)–(4) from 16 ns NVE molecular dynamics simulations of one NV center in a diamond cell of 10648 atoms. We com- pare the numerical results of T1 (blue circles) with our exper- imental results (purple stars), and with those of Ref. [2] (red star), Ref. [42] (orange stars) and Ref. [40] (green star). We omit the result at 1000 K, presented in Ref. [3], as measure- ments were conducted on nanodiamonds, where relaxation mechanisms may substantially differ from those in bulk di- amond.
